Settlement.name: {=!}Fatehpur Sikri
Settlement.text: {=!}Built by Emperor Akbar in 1571 as his grand new capital, Fatehpur Sikri stands as a masterpiece of Mughal architecture blending Persian, Hindu, and Jain styles in glowing red sandstone. The fortress-city was abandoned barely fourteen years after its completion when its water supply proved inadequate for a great court. Its empty halls still whisper the ambitions of the empire's greatest builder. It now serves as a commanding citadel overseeing the road between Agra and the north.

Settlement.name: {=!}Qila e Aligarh
Settlement.text: {=!}The formidable Qila e Aligarh squats above the confluence of the Angan and Nim rivers, a bulwark that has changed hands between Afghan chiefs, Rajput clans, and Mughal governors over many generations. Its thick walls and wide ditches make it one of the most defensible positions in the Doab. Those who hold this fort hold the keys to the fertile plain between the Ganga and Yamuna. Its garrison is considered vital to the security of the imperial heartland.

Settlement.name: {=!}Sambhal
Settlement.text: {=!}Sambhal's ancient fortifications crown a ridge above the dusty plains of Rohilkhand, commanding the crossroads between Delhi and the eastern territories. The city is ancient beyond reckoning, its stones layered with the memory of successive empires. Local tradition claims it as the birthplace of the future Kalki avatar, lending the fort a spiritual significance that makes its garrison doubly fearless. The Mughal governor here rules more as a local king than a distant administrator.

Settlement.name: {=!}Meerut
Settlement.text: {=!}One of the oldest citadels on the subcontinent, Meerut's fort oversees a city where armies have marched since the days of the Mahabharata. The current battlements date to the Delhi Sultanate, repaired and augmented by every subsequent power. Its position on the road north from Delhi makes it the first line of defense against any invasion from the hill country. The garrison here is traditionally composed of veteran infantry who have learned to hold walls against charging cavalry.

Settlement.name: {=!}Kanpur
Settlement.text: {=!}Kanpur's riverside fort commands the most important ford on the Ganga between Allahabad and the north, making it indispensable to any power wishing to move armies or trade goods across the great river. The current structure is modest compared to the grand fortresses further upstream, but its position compensates for its size. The nearby ghats serve both as landing points for river craft and as a place of pilgrimage, so the fort garrison must balance military duty with managing the flow of holy men and merchants.

Settlement.name: {=!}Kannauj
Settlement.text: {=!}Kannauj sits atop the memory of three successive medieval empires, each of which considered this fort-city the ultimate prize of Hindustan. The Gurjara-Pratiharas, the Palas, and the Rashtrakutas all fought wars that lasted generations for its possession, and the Afghans took it to prove they owned India. Now a shadow of its former glory, the fort still commands the highest ground above the Ganga-Kali confluence. Its name alone still carries the weight of legend in the bazaars of Delhi.

Settlement.name: {=!}Etawah
Settlement.text: {=!}Etawah's hilltop citadel juts above a sharp bend in the Yamuna, where Rajput chiefs once waged endless war against Mughal authority before finally being crushed by Akbar's generals. The fort is built partly from the ruins of earlier structures, and arrowheads and broken blades still surface after the monsoon rains. The local commanders have a reputation for independence that their nominal masters in Delhi find perpetually irritating. Controlling Etawah means controlling the central river crossing between Agra and the east.

Settlement.name: {=!}Moradabad
Settlement.text: {=!}Murad Bakhsh, a prince of the Mughal blood, founded this fort on the banks of the Ramganga, and it bears his name in honour of that lineage. Moradabad has grown into a prosperous town famous for its metalwork, and the fort's revenues flow from taxes on the craftsmen who hammer brass and copper in the bazaars below. The garrison is relatively light for a fort of its size, as the region is domesticated and prosperous. It serves primarily as a headquarters for administering the surrounding Rohilkhand countryside.

Settlement.name: {=!}Tughlaqabad
Settlement.text: {=!}Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq built this massive fortress in 1321 to prove he ruled the world, ringed by a wall of sloping granite designed to foil any escalade. But the curse of the sufi Nizamuddin Auliya fell upon him, and the king died before he could inhabit his creation. Now Tughlaqabad is a haunt for jackals and wandering ascetics, its great chambers open to the sky. Yet its walls remain thick and serviceable, and any commander who garrisons these ruins holds a position of remarkable natural strength.

Settlement.name: {=!}Akbarabad
Settlement.text: {=!}Built by Akbar on the banks of the Yamuna, Akbarabad was once the jewel of the Mughal Empire. The Red Fort and the incomparable Taj Mahal bear witness to the days when it stood as the greatest city in the world. Though the court has shifted to Shahjahanabad, its workshops still produce the finest inlaid stonework and carpets in the realm.

Settlement.name: {=!}Shahjahanabad
Settlement.text: {=!}Built by Shah Jahan as the seventh city of Delhi, Shahjahanabad is the seat of the Mughal Emperor and the heart of a civilization. The Red Fort rises above the Yamuna like a crimson mountain while the Jama Masjid watches over bazaars trading in silk and spice. Yet beneath its grandeur the city seethes with factional intrigue as nobles scheme in the shadow of a weakening throne.

Settlement.name: {=!}Allahabad
Settlement.text: {=!}Where the sacred Ganga meets the Yamuna and the mythical Saraswati, Allahabad has been a place of pilgrimage for millennia. The Mughal fortress guarding the sangam commands the rivers and controls the most important pilgrimage routes of the north. Every twelve years the Kumbh Mela draws millions of the faithful, briefly making it the most populated place on earth.

Settlement.name: {=!}Lucknow
Settlement.text: {=!}Rising in the fertile plains of Awadh, Lucknow is a city of poets, musicians, and scholars where Persian high culture has found perhaps its finest expression in all of India. Its bazaars are renowned for the finest fabrics, perfumes, and cuisine on the subcontinent. The Nawabs who govern it have turned the city into a paradox - a place of extraordinary elegance maintained through careful management of Mughal patronage.

Settlement.name: {=!}Mathura
Settlement.text: {=!}Among the most sacred cities in the Hindu faith, Mathura stands as the birthplace of Lord Krishna on the banks of the Yamuna. Its ghats are perpetually crowded with pilgrims seeking liberation and its temples adorned with carvings of incomparable beauty. The Mughal governors have learned to tread carefully here, for any insult to its sanctity risks stirring a religious uprising that gold alone cannot buy off.

Settlement.name: {=!}Bareilly
Settlement.text: {=!}The principal market town of the Rohilkhand plateau, Bareilly has grown rich from trade flowing between the Himalayan foothills and the Gangetic plains. Afghan Rohilla mercenaries have settled in the region in growing numbers, giving the town a martial character unusual for a commercial center. Control of Bareilly means control of the mountain passes and the lucrative timber and spice routes beyond.

Settlement.name: {=!}Gaur Fort
Settlement.text: {=!}Gaur was once the City of Gold, capital of the Bengal Sultanate and one of the largest cities in the medieval world, its towers visible from leagues away. Repeated floods, famines, and conquests have broken it, and the jungle has reclaimed much of what dynasties built. The remaining fortifications are impressive ruins still capable of housing a strong garrison among their crumbling halls. Walking its empty streets, a soldier can feel the weight of vanished centuries pressing down like the Bengal humidity.

Settlement.name: {=!}Rajmahal Fort
Settlement.text: {=!}Rajmahal served as the Mughal capital of Bengal under Akbar, selected for its commanding position above the Ganges where the river bends close to the Rajmahal hills. Emperor Islam Khan later moved the capital downriver to Dhaka, leaving Rajmahal as a forward garrison post rather than an administrative center. The fort's elevated riverside position gives its artillery an excellent field of fire over river traffic. It remains the most important military post between Bihar and the delta.

Settlement.name: {=!}Burdwan Fort
Settlement.text: {=!}The fortified seat of the Burdwan Raj stands in the fertile heart of Bengal, its revenues drawn from the most productive rice lands in the subcontinent. The Maharaja of Burdwan has grown rich administering the zamindar system for the Nawabs of Bengal, and his fortified house reflects a merchant-lord's prosperity rather than a warrior king's austerity. The surrounding countryside is so densely populated and agriculturally productive that whoever holds Burdwan holds Bengal's granary. The garrison here is comfortable rather than hardened.

Settlement.name: {=!}Rohtasgarh
Settlement.text: {=!}Rohtasgarh crouches on a five-mile mesa above the Son River in Bihar, its sheer cliffs making it effectively impregnable to direct assault. Sher Shah Suri used it as his treasury, and the Mughals maintained it as a refuge of last resort. Access is possible only through four narrow gates, each cut into cliff faces that funnel attackers into killzones. The fort's granaries and cisterns can sustain a large garrison through years of siege, making it the most defensible position in the entire Gangetic plain.

Settlement.name: {=!}Midnapore Fort
Settlement.text: {=!}Midnapore sits at the edge of the red laterite soil country where Bengal grades into Orissa, a frontier zone where the Nawab's writ has always been more notional than real. The fort garrison spends as much time negotiating with local zamindars and tribal chiefs as it does conducting military operations. The jungle country to the south makes it impossible to impose central authority by force alone. Whoever administers Midnapore must learn to govern through a web of local relationships rather than direct command.

Settlement.name: {=!}Sylhet Fort
Settlement.text: {=!}Sylhet's fortress guards the eastern gateway of Bengal where the river valleys open toward Assam, Tripura, and the distant hills of Manipur. The region is subject to periodic raids from the hill chieftains and the Ahom kingdom, and the garrison must remain perpetually alert despite the lush beauty of the surrounding tea country. The local population is a mixture of Bengali Muslims and Hindu hill peoples, making administration here a delicate exercise in religious tolerance. The fort's position at the confluence of the Surma and Kushiyara rivers gives it control of the eastern river network.

Settlement.name: {=!}Purnea Fort
Settlement.text: {=!}Purnea commands the flat plain where the Gandak and Kosi rivers converge, guarding the corridors between Bengal, Bihar, and the Himalayan foothills of Nepal. The fort is modest but strategically essential, as any army moving between eastern Bengal and the north must either cross rivers here or face a long detour. The surrounding countryside is rich in sugarcane and indigo, and the garrison's revenues from the export trade keep it well-supplied. In the monsoon, when the rivers flood, the fort becomes an island in a sea of silt.

Settlement.name: {=!}Shah Sarai Fort
Settlement.text: {=!}Shah Sarai began as a fortified caravanserai on the old Mughal highway between Patna and Varanasi, expanded over generations into a proper fortified strongpoint. The Sher Shah Suri Road passes directly beneath its walls, making it an indispensable customs post and relay station for imperial communications. Its barracks, stables, and warehouses can support the movement of entire armies along this critical artery. Control of Shah Sarai means control of Bihar's commerce.

Settlement.name: {=!}Dacca
Settlement.text: {=!}The old capital of Mughal Bengal, Dacca remains the greatest city of the delta, famous for the production of the finest muslin in the world - a cloth so sheer that Mughal ladies called it woven air. Its waterways are perpetually crowded with boats laden with goods for markets as distant as Venice and Canton. Despite its commercial dominance, the city's power is entirely dependent on the prosperity of its looms.

Settlement.name: {=!}Murshidabad
Settlement.text: {=!}Built by Murshid Quli Khan to replace the old Mughal capital at Dacca, Murshidabad is the nerve center of the wealthiest province in the empire. The Nawab's treasury here is so vast that the banking houses of the Jagat Seths have become powers in their own right. Every grain merchant, silk trader, and indigo dealer east of the Yamuna must eventually bow to Murshidabad.

Settlement.name: {=!}Hooghly
Settlement.text: {=!}The port at Hooghly where the Dutch and Portuguese first established their trading posts is the door through which European goods, money, and increasingly guns enter Bengal. The Portuguese church and the Dutch trading house stand within sight of each other, their flags a reminder that Bengal's wealth has drawn competitors from the far ends of the earth. Control of Hooghly means control of Bengal's access to global trade.

Settlement.name: {=!}Patna
Settlement.text: {=!}The ancient city of Pataliputra, once the capital of the Mauryan and Gupta empires, is now the Mughal provincial capital of Bihar and one of the greatest trading cities on the Ganga. Its opium fields supply the vast Chinese market through the coastal ports, making it a city where enormous fortunes are built and lost with every harvest season. The river traffic through Patna is the lifeblood of the entire Gangetic plain.

Settlement.name: {=!}Chittagong
Settlement.text: {=!}The great eastern port where Bengali, Persian, and Burmese merchants meet, Chittagong's harbor shelters ships from Arabia, Persia, Southeast Asia, and the Portuguese Estado da India simultaneously. The city's position at the mouth of the hill country makes it the natural outlet for the timber, cotton, and spices of the deep interior. Whoever holds Chittagong controls the eastern sea-trade of the entire Bay of Bengal.

Settlement.name: {=!}Cuttack
Settlement.text: {=!}The gateway to Orissa, Cuttack is built on a tongue of land between two rivers that makes it both naturally defensible and a natural hub of river trade. The temples of the Jagannath cult fill the city and the surrounding district, drawing pilgrims from across the subcontinent. Control of Cuttack means control of the rich Orissan coast and the elephant-hunting grounds of the interior forests.

Settlement.name: {=!}Golconda Fort
Settlement.text: {=!}Golconda Fort rose from a shepherd's rock into the wealthiest fortress in Asia, its vaults once brimming with the diamonds of the Kollur mines including the famed Koh-i-Noor. The Qutb Shahi sultans ruled from here for nearly two centuries before Aurangzeb's armies spent eight months hammering its walls until a traitor opened a gate. Its acoustics are legendary - a clap at the gate can be heard at the summit. The fort now lies scarred but still formidable, its steep slopes a nightmare for any attacking infantry.

Settlement.name: {=!}Daulatabad
Settlement.text: {=!}Daulatabad perches atop a sheer-sided volcanic plug rising two hundred feet from the Deccan plain, surrounded by a deep moat and a labyrinthine tunnel passage that can be smoked to suffocate intruders. Muhammad bin Tughlaq renamed it from Devagiri and tried to move the entire population of Delhi here, an act of madness that killed thousands in the desert march. Aurangzeb later used it as a prison for the Maratha king Sambhaji's family. No fort on the Deccan has been taken by direct assault.

Settlement.name: {=!}Naldurg Fort
Settlement.text: {=!}Naldurg straddles the Bori River with a dam that can be raised to flood the approaches, creating a moat of variable depth that confounds sieges. The Portuguese engineer its water defenses in the service of the Bijapur Sultanate, a rare meeting of Deccan and European military thinking. Its location on the Osmanabad plateau makes it the key to controlling movement between the Marathwada and the northern Deccan. The fort's remarkable acoustics carry the muezzin's call to villages miles distant.

Settlement.name: {=!}Udgir Fort
Settlement.text: {=!}Udgir's hilltop fort commands the plateau roads of Marathwada, built by the Bahmani Sultanate and contested between its successor states for generations. The Treaty of Udgir in 1759, where the Marathas humiliated the Nizam's forces, has given this modest fort an outsized political significance. Its walls are not especially impressive, but the surrounding terrain is open and offers excellent sight lines for miles in every direction. Commanders here have the luxury of seeing their enemies coming long before they arrive.

Settlement.name: {=!}Mahur Fort
Settlement.text: {=!}Mahur is sacred as the birthplace of the goddess Renuka, and its hilltop fort rises beside the ancient temple complex overlooking the Penganga River. Pilgrims and soldiers share the steep path to the summit, creating an uneasy coexistence of the holy and the martial. The Bahmani Sultanate built the current defenses, but the site was fortified long before Islam came to the Deccan. Any commander who would raze its temples to improve the field of fire must first reckon with the fury of the goddess's devotees.

Settlement.name: {=!}Parenda Fort
Settlement.text: {=!}Parenda's squat bastion of mud and dressed stone dates to the Bahmani era, its thick walls designed to absorb cannon fire rather than deflect it. It sits at a critical crossroads in the Osmanabad plateau, controlling movement between the northern Deccan and the Marathwada granaries. The fort contains several deep wells that ensure a water supply even in the worst siege. Its architecture is functional rather than grand, built by men who understood that a plain wall that holds is worth more than an ornate one that falls.

Settlement.name: {=!}Kaulas Fort
Settlement.text: {=!}Kaulas sits above the Manjra River where it bends east toward Hyderabad, a modest but well-watered stronghold that has served as a supply depot and forward base for armies marching between the two great Deccan powers. Its fortifications are unremarkable, but its orchards and cisterns can sustain a garrison of several hundred through any siege. The local population is a mixture of Maratha peasant-farmers and Telugu merchants, loyal to whoever pays their taxes and leaves their harvests intact.

Settlement.name: {=!}Medak Fort
Settlement.text: {=!}Medak Fort rises from a granite hill above the plains of Telangana, its double walls enclosing a self-sufficient fortress city that stored grain for years of siege. The Portuguese influenced its construction through their dealings with the Qutb Shahi kingdom, and some bastions show European-style outworks. It guards the northern approaches to Hyderabad with implacable solidity. The local garrison has a tradition of independent command, as no relief force has ever arrived in time when Medak was besieged.

Settlement.name: {=!}Warangal
Settlement.text: {=!}The ancient capital of the Kakatiya kings, Warangal is a city of temples and ruined fortresses that once commanded the entire Deccan plateau. Its famous fortress of a thousand pillars is crumbling now, but the city remains a vital crossroads for trade between the coast and the interior. The Nizam governors have worked to restore its ancient glory as a symbol of the legitimacy of their rule.

Settlement.name: {=!}Aurangabad
Settlement.text: {=!}Named for the emperor who made it his capital during the long Deccan campaigns, Aurangabad is a city forged by military will. The formidable Daulatabad Fort looms over the surrounding plains from its isolated volcanic rock, virtually impregnable. Though the Mughal armies have departed northward the city retains the infrastructure of empire - workshops, granaries, and arsenals that the Nizam has gratefully inherited.

Settlement.name: {=!}Bidar
Settlement.text: {=!}The jewel of the Bahmani Sultanate and later the capital of the Barid Shahi dynasty, Bidar's magnificent fort and elaborately tiled tombs speak of centuries of Islamic learning and art. The Bidar School of metalwork - a unique technique of inlaying silver into blackened steel - remains famous across the subcontinent. Though its days of imperial glory are past the city remains a center of Deccani culture and Sufi scholarship.

Settlement.name: {=!}Hyderabad
Settlement.text: {=!}Founded barely a century ago on the banks of the Musi, Hyderabad has grown with stunning speed into one of the most magnificent cities in India. The Charminar stands at its center like a four-armed sentinel watching over bazaars that trade in pearls, diamonds, and silks from every corner of the world. Under Asaf Jah it has become the administrative capital of a new Deccan order, efficient and purposeful where other great cities have grown lazy.

Settlement.name: {=!}Burhanpur
Settlement.text: {=!}Straddling the Tapti river at the gateway between Mughal India and the Deccan, Burhanpur has long been the staging ground for every army that has marched south. Its textile workshops produce the finest cotton muslin in the empire, so prized that European merchants call it Burhanpuri cloth. Its position on the border of competing powers makes it perpetually contested territory.

Settlement.name: {=!}Elichpur
Settlement.text: {=!}One of the oldest cities in the Deccan, Elichpur has passed through the hands of a dozen rulers and remembers the glory of the old Bahmani Sultanate. Situated where the plateau descends toward the Vidarbha plains, it commands rich cotton-growing lands and lucrative textile markets. The local nobles are proud of their city's antiquity and do not easily submit to outside authority.

Settlement.name: {=!}Golconda
Settlement.text: {=!}The ancient fortress-city of Golconda rises from a granite hill above the plains of Telangana, its concentric walls enclosing the old Qutb Shahi capital that once commanded the richest diamond trade in the world. The Koh-i-Noor, the Hope, and the Regent all emerged from mines in the Krishna valley below this fortress, and the gem-cutters of Golconda became so synonymous with wealth that the city's name passed into common speech as a byword for limitless riches. Aurangzeb took Golconda after a long siege in 1687, ending a century of Qutb Shahi splendor, but the bazaars still glitter with the finest carnelian, beryl, and worked gold in the Deccan. The Hyderabad Nizam now holds these walls, and his soldiers guard a treasury built on the memory of diamonds.

Settlement.name: {=!}Bala Hissar
Settlement.text: {=!}Bala Hissar has towered above Kabul since before memory, its mud-brick walls rebuilt by every conqueror who passed through the Hindu Kush on their way to India or retreat from it. Babur fortified it when he made Kabul his first kingdom, and his successors used it as the treasury of empire. The fort commands the entire Kabul valley from its rocky promontory, and any army approaching from the north or west falls under its guns for hours before reaching the city. To hold Bala Hissar is to hold Kabul.

Settlement.name: {=!}Khyber Pass
Settlement.text: {=!}The Khyber Pass is not a fort so much as a fortress of geography, thirty miles of narrow defile through the Hindu Kush that every army invading India from the northwest has navigated while the defenders rained rocks and arrows from above. Small forts and watchtowers line the crags, manned by Afridi tribesmen who have been bought, bribed, or fought into cooperation with whichever power currently controls Peshawar. The pass cannot be held by one side alone; it is always negotiated. Control of the Khyber means controlling the gate between Central Asia and the subcontinent.

Settlement.name: {=!}Bamyan
Settlement.text: {=!}Bamyan's fortress crouches in a high-altitude valley once famous for the two gigantic Buddha statues carved from its sandstone cliffs, now rubble after Mongol fury. The remaining fortifications command the central Hindu Kush road, the ancient Silk Road route through the mountains connecting Kabul with Balkh. At eight thousand feet, the winters are brutal and the garrison must be sustained through summer caravans before the passes close. Bamyan has been attacked, abandoned, and reoccupied by a dozen empires, each leaving traces in its stratified walls.

Settlement.name: {=!}Farah
Settlement.text: {=!}Farah's mud-brick fortress rises from the dry steppe of western Afghanistan, where the desert grades toward the edge of Persia. It commands the junction of the routes from Herat, Kandahar, and the Sistan basin, making it the crossroads of western Afghanistan's sparse but vital trade. The garrison here lives in heat that cracks the walls and cold that freezes the wells, serving on what most Afghan warriors consider a posting fit only for punishment. Yet Farah has strategic value beyond its comfort, and great armies have fought over its waterless approaches.

Settlement.name: {=!}Lashkar Gah
Settlement.text: {=!}Lashkar Gah preserves the memory of Mahmud of Ghazna's winter palace complex on the Helmand River, where the great conqueror rested between his seventeen raids into India. The current fort incorporates ruins of those palatial buildings, their carved stucco panels a ghost of vanished luxury. The Helmand's water sustains agriculture in an otherwise arid region, and controlling Lashkar Gah means controlling the most fertile corridor in Kandahar province. Its garrison must simultaneously manage the water rights of farmers and the toll roads of the desert.

Settlement.name: {=!}Kunduz
Settlement.text: {=!}Kunduz guards the fertile river valleys at the foot of the Hindu Kush where the Kunduz River descends from the mountains toward the Amu Darya. The surrounding cotton and grain farms make it the most prosperous posting in northern Afghanistan, and the garrison grows fat on market taxes. The fort itself is substantial, rebuilt by the Timurid princes who fought over Transoxiana's wealth. It serves as the main waystation for caravans moving between Kabul and Bukhara through the northern passes.

Settlement.name: {=!}Ghor
Settlement.text: {=!}Ghor is the ancestral homeland of the Ghorid dynasty that once ruled from the Atlantic to Bengal, now a remote mountain citadel accessible only through passes that close for months in winter. The isolation that made it a refuge for rebels also makes it nearly impossible to supply a large garrison. Local Tajik and Hazara chieftains pay nominal loyalty to whoever holds the fort while continuing their ancient practices undisturbed. Ghor is a place where the writ of distant empires fades into the thin mountain air.

Settlement.name: {=!}Zaranj
Settlement.text: {=!}Zaranj guards the ancient Sistan basin where the Helmand River fans into a vast lake before disappearing into the desert, the crossroads between Persia, Kandahar, and the Makran coast. The Saffarid dynasty was born here in the ninth century, one of the first Persian Muslim dynasties, and the fort carries that heritage in every course of its weathered brick. The surrounding region is subject to the dreaded wind of a hundred and twenty days, a seasonal gale that drives sand through every crack and maddens men who stay too long. Yet Zaranj's water access and its position on the Persian trade route make it too valuable to abandon.

Settlement.name: {=!}Kabul
Settlement.text: {=!}Perched high in the Hindu Kush at the crossroads of the ancient Silk Road, Kabul is a city of stark beauty and brutal winters. Since the time of Babur, who called it his paradise, the city has served as the gateway between Central Asia and the riches of India. Its bazaars are the meeting point of Persian, Indian, and Central Asian trade, and its massive citadel commands every road that leads south through the mountain passes.

Settlement.name: {=!}Ghazni
Settlement.text: {=!}Built on a windswept plateau at the edge of the Afghan highlands, Ghazni was once the capital of the greatest empire in the Islamic world, when Sultan Mahmud launched his famous raids into the wealth of India from this very stronghold. The tombs of the Ghaznavid sultans still draw pilgrims from across the Muslim world. Its modern importance lies in its position as the midpoint of the road between Kabul and Kandahar.

Settlement.name: {=!}Peshawar
Settlement.text: {=!}Where the mountains give way to the Indus plains, Peshawar guards the mouth of the Khyber Pass. It is the last great city of the Afghan world and the first of the Indian one - a place where every culture, religion, and language mingles freely in its markets. The Pathan tribes of the surrounding hills are a constant factor in the city's politics, powerful allies or terrible enemies depending on how generously they are treated.

Settlement.name: {=!}Jalalabad
Settlement.text: {=!}Controlling the road between Kabul and the Khyber Pass, Jalalabad sits in a warm valley that is the gateway between the high Hindu Kush and the plains of the Indus. Its mild winters make it a preferred residence for Afghan rulers and its orchards of pomegranate and orange are the most productive in the region. Any army moving between India and Central Asia must pass within cannon-shot of its walls.

Settlement.name: {=!}Balkh
Settlement.text: {=!}Ancient Bactria, called the Mother of Cities, Balkh stands at the northern edge of the Afghan world. Once one of the greatest cities in Asia, it has been repeatedly sacked and rebuilt, each time rising from its own ruins. The shrine of Hazrat Ali - the mosque that tradition holds was built by the fourth Caliph - makes it the most sacred city in Afghanistan and a seat of immense religious authority.

Settlement.name: {=!}Herat
Settlement.text: {=!}Blessed with fertile soil and a long tradition of Persian learning, Herat was for centuries the capital of the Timurid renaissance. Its libraries once held the finest manuscripts in the Islamic world and its painters and poets were studied from Istanbul to Beijing. Though much reduced, it remains a center of Persian civilization on the eastern frontier, a city that remembers when Afghanistan was the heart of an empire.

Settlement.name: {=!}Kandahar
Settlement.text: {=!}Historically disputed between the Persian Safavids and the Mughal emperors, Kandahar sits at the southern junction of all roads connecting Central Asia, India, and Persia. It is now the home base of Ahmad Shah Durrani and the founding capital of the new Afghan confederacy. The city's massive citadel and its position controlling three great trade routes make it the most strategically important city in the Afghan world.

Settlement.name: {=!}Devanahalli
Settlement.text: {=!}Devanahalli is doubly sacred to the Mysore kingdom, for it is not only a formidable hilltop fort commanding the northern approach to the Deccan plateau, but also the birthplace of the great general Hyder Ali himself. The fort's current master therefore rules the birthplace of the man who rebuilt Mysore from a petty chieftaincy into a regional power. The surrounding country is dry and open, giving defenders excellent visibility over any approaching force. The garrison takes pride in defending the place that produced their kingdom's greatest son.

Settlement.name: {=!}Nandi Hills
Settlement.text: {=!}Nandi Hills rises two thousand feet above the surrounding plain, its misty summit the summer retreat where Hyder Ali escaped the punishing Deccan heat with his court and treasury. The fort perched on the summit is virtually inaccessible; its single approach road runs along a ridge narrow enough that two men cannot walk abreast. The cool air and permanent springs make it the most pleasant posting in the Mysore military, and the view from the ramparts encompasses an impossible expanse of the Karnataka plateau. Armies have besieged the hill and given up without reaching the walls.

Settlement.name: {=!}Savandurga
Settlement.text: {=!}Savandurga rises from two enormous peaks of smooth granite that erupt from the Karnataka plateau, their sheer faces offering no purchase for scaling ladders or siege equipment. The fort occupies the saddle between the peaks, accessible only through a single defended path cut into the rock. The Gangas and later the Vijayanagara Empire held this place before Mysore, and each left additions to its defenses. Capturing Savandurga requires either starvation or treachery; no army has taken it by assault.

Settlement.name: {=!}Madhugiri
Settlement.text: {=!}Madhugiri's fortifications crown what is believed to be the second-largest monolithic rock in Asia, its smooth granite dome rising abruptly from the flat Karnataka plain with no foothills to ease the approach. The single path to the summit passes through seven successive gates, each of which must be forced before the next can be attacked. Hyder Ali himself besieged this fort for months before the garrison surrendered from thirst rather than defeat. The surrounding countryside grows ragi and cotton for the Mysore markets, making the fort's revenues respectable despite its remote location.

Settlement.name: {=!}Dindigul
Settlement.text: {=!}Dindigul Fort stands on a granite hill above the town that guards the gateway between the Kaveri delta and Coimbatore, one of the most strategically vital corridors in the Tamil south. Hyder Ali used it as his base for campaigns into the Tamil country, and the fort's excellent repair reflects his careful maintenance of defensive works. The surrounding region grows excellent coffee and the Dindigul locks made in the town below are famous for their quality. Its garrison must watch both the eastern plains and the western ghat passes.

Settlement.name: {=!}Palakkad Fort
Settlement.text: {=!}Palakkad Fort commands the only broad gap through the Western Ghats, the Palakkad Pass that connects the Malabar Coast with the Tamil plains. Any army moving between Kerala and Tamil Nadu must pass beneath its guns. The fort was originally built by Hyder Ali when Mysore seized the pass from the Zamorin of Calicut, and it has been a permanent flashpoint between Mysore and its coastal rivals ever since. Whoever holds Palakkad controls trade between the pepper gardens of Malabar and the cotton markets of the Coromandel.

Settlement.name: {=!}Krishnagiri
Settlement.text: {=!}Krishnagiri's hilltop fort commands the passes that link the Deccan plateau with the Tamil country, a chokepoint that armies and merchants have navigated since the days of the Chola empire. The surrounding hills are forested and difficult, making the fort difficult to invest completely. Hyder Ali's forces seized it from the Nawab of the Carnatic, and Mysore has held it as a forward post against encroachment from the north and east. The garrison here is accustomed to independent operations, receiving orders from Seringapatam only when the postal roads are clear.

Settlement.name: {=!}Bellary
Settlement.text: {=!}Bellary's twin-peaked rocky citadel dominates the flat iron-ore country of the Deccan's eastern frontier, its rust-red stone blending into the iron-rich landscape. The Vijayanagara Empire built it as a defense against the Bahmani Sultanate, and successive powers have garrisoned it ever since. The surrounding region is rich in iron ore and cotton, making the fort a center of local industry as well as military power. Bellary marks the furthest extent of Mysore's northern ambitions on the Deccan plateau.

Settlement.name: {=!}Gurramkonda
Settlement.text: {=!}Gurramkonda perches above the Telugu frontier between Mysore and Hyderabad, a modest fort of considerable strategic importance at the intersection of hill country and plain. The surrounding region speaks Telugu and maintains customs distinct from either the Kannada culture of Mysore or the Persian culture of Hyderabad. The garrison here acts as much as administrators of a mixed population as it does as soldiers. Control of Gurramkonda is often the subject of diplomatic disputes between Mysore and Hyderabad that stop just short of open war.

Settlement.name: {=!}Srirangapatna
Settlement.text: {=!}The island fortress capital of Mysore, ringed by the waters of the Kaveri on three sides, Srirangapatna was chosen as the seat of power precisely because it is nearly invulnerable. The great Ranganathaswamy temple at its heart draws pilgrims from across South India, its towers visible from leagues away. The Wadiyar kings have governed from this sacred island for generations, using its natural defenses to hold off enemies who would otherwise have consumed their modest kingdom.

Settlement.name: {=!}Chitradurga
Settlement.text: {=!}A chain of seven concentric fortifications built into the granite outcroppings of a natural hill makes Chitradurga one of the most formidable strongholds in the Deccan. The polygar chiefs who built it relied on rough terrain as much as any man-made wall. Controlling Chitradurga means controlling the high road between the Deccan plateau and the fertile Karnataka plains below.

Settlement.name: {=!}Bednore
Settlement.text: {=!}Tucked into the foothills where the Western Ghats descend to the coast, Bednore commands the mountain passes linking the sea ports to the interior of Karnataka. Its mines produce silver and its forests the finest teak in India. The Keladi Nayakas who rule here have successfully maintained their independence by playing Mysore and the coastal powers against each other.

Settlement.name: {=!}Coimbatore
Settlement.text: {=!}Sitting in the Palghat Pass - the only easy route through the Western Ghats - Coimbatore controls the single most important land corridor connecting the east and west coasts of South India. Every army that has ever marched across the southern peninsula has passed through or fought for this valley. Its productive cotton fields make it as valuable commercially as it is strategically.

Settlement.name: {=!}Mangalore
Settlement.text: {=!}The most important port on the western coast of Karnataka, Mangalore's natural harbor shelters Arab dhows, Chinese junks, and European merchant vessels that have made it a crossroads of the Indian Ocean trade for a thousand years. The city's spice merchants trade pepper and cardamom from the surrounding hills for horses, gold, and weapons from Arabia and Persia. Holding Mangalore means holding Karnataka's door to the world's sea-lanes.

Settlement.name: {=!}Bangalore
Settlement.text: {=!}Founded as a military cantonment to guard the passes between the Karnataka plateau and the Kaveri valley, Bangalore has grown into a thriving market town famous for its pleasant climate and silk-weaving workshops. The Kempe Gowda fort that guards the town is being steadily improved as Mysore's power expands. Its central position in the Karnataka highlands makes it the natural administrative heart of the region.

Settlement.name: {=!}Mysore
Settlement.text: {=!}The ancient seat of the Wadiyar dynasty, Mysore gives its name to the entire kingdom. The city's palace is one of the grandest in South India and the ten-day Dasara festival held here is the most magnificent royal spectacle in the south. The Chamundeswari temple atop the hill overlooking the city is the tutelary goddess of the royal family, and no monarch rules Mysore without her blessing.

Settlement.name: {=!}Sira
Settlement.text: {=!}The gateway between Mysore and the Bijapur plateau, the fort of Sira has historically served as the Mughal administrative center for the far south. Positioned at the junction of several important trade roads, whoever holds Sira controls access between the Muslim Deccan and the Hindu south. The city's importance has made it a perpetual prize in the wars between Mysore and the Deccan powers.

Settlement.name: {=!}Chittorgarh
Settlement.text: {=!}Chittorgarh sprawls across a seven-mile plateau above the Rajputana plains, the greatest Rajput citadel and the shrine of Rajput honour through three sackings that each ended in the rite of jauhar, where the women of the fort walked into fire rather than accept captivity. Alauddin Khalji, Bahadur Shah of Gujarat, and Akbar himself took these walls, yet the Sisodia clan returned each time. The fort contains palaces, temples, step-wells, and towers that represent centuries of Rajput culture accumulated in stone. No other fort in India carries such weight of memory and loss.

Settlement.name: {=!}Kumbhalgarh
Settlement.text: {=!}Kumbhalgarh's walls stretch for thirty-six miles around the summit of the Aravalli hills, the second longest wall in the world, enclosing fifteen thousand square miles of forest and farmland within its defenses. Maharana Kumbha built it in the fifteenth century atop a site fortified since ancient times, and it has never fallen to direct assault in its entire history. Maharana Pratap was born within its walls during the long resistance against Akbar. The fort is visible from fifty miles away in clear weather, its battlements a jagged crown on the ridge of the Aravallis.

Settlement.name: {=!}Ranthambore
Settlement.text: {=!}Ranthambore rises from a jungle-covered hill deep in the wild country where tigers still roam the ravines, its walls emerging from the forest as if grown from the rock. Alauddin Khalji besieged it for years and eventually took it through exhaustion of the garrison's supplies rather than force of arms. The jungle makes it impossible to invest completely, and small parties can always slip through the forest to resupply or escape. Ranthambore is considered unconquerable by assault, and any commander assigned to take it by force has the sympathy of his peers.

Settlement.name: {=!}Mehrangarh
Settlement.text: {=!}Mehrangarh towers above the Blue City of Jodhpur, its walls rising straight from a sheer rock face that falls three hundred feet to the plain below. The Rathore clan built it as the permanent expression of their dominance over the Marwar desert, and its galleries, courtyards, and chambers reflect centuries of Rathore patronage of the arts. The cannon balls still visible lodged in its gates are reminders that even this fortress was attacked, though never taken. From its ramparts, the entire blue expanse of Jodhpur spreads below, every building painted in the colour of the Brahmins who inhabited the old city.

Settlement.name: {=!}Junagarh
Settlement.text: {=!}Junagarh stands in the middle of Bikaner, a lowland fortress without a hill to stand on, relying entirely on the ingenuity of its design and the ferocity of its garrison. Built in 1593 by Rai Singh, one of Akbar's most celebrated generals, it has never been conquered in battle in its entire existence. The fort is a series of palaces within palaces, each courtyard more elaborately decorated than the last, financed by the wealth of the Bikaner camel trade across the Thar Desert. The garrison here guards the city itself as much as any frontier.

Settlement.name: {=!}Gagron
Settlement.text: {=!}Gagron is unique among Rajput forts in being surrounded by water on three sides, where the Ahu and Kali Sindh rivers meet to create a natural double moat below its cliff face. The Khichi Chauhan clan held it for seven generations before the Sultans of Malwa forced two episodes of jauhar from its walls. The current Rajput lords maintain it as a testament to what their ancestors endured. Its position above two rivers makes it impossible to take by storm, and its natural water supply makes sieges very long affairs.

Settlement.name: {=!}Taragarh
Settlement.text: {=!}Taragarh, the Star Fort, crowns the hill above Ajmer that guards the pass through the Aravalli range connecting Rajputana with the Gangetic plain. No army can march between Delhi and Ajmer without passing under its guns, making it perhaps the most strategically significant fort in all of Rajputana. Ana Sagar lake spreads below it on one side, its water feeding the fort's cisterns through an ancient aqueduct. The Dargah of Moinuddin Chishti below makes Ajmer equally sacred to Muslims and Hindus, and the garrison must manage pilgrims alongside enemies.

Settlement.name: {=!}Nagaur
Settlement.text: {=!}Nagaur Fort sits in the desert between Jodhpur and Bikaner, a vast enclosed citadel of orange-red sandstone that has been held by Mughals, Rajputs, and local chiefs in bewildering succession. Akbar held court here, Aurangzeb imprisoned his brother Murad within its walls, and the Rathores have claimed it more times than memory serves. The fort encloses a small town within its walls and contains the oldest surviving well-pulley system in Rajputana. Its disputed ownership between neighboring Rajput clans makes it perpetually contested.

Settlement.name: {=!}Ajmer
Settlement.text: {=!}Built around the dargah of the Sufi saint Moinuddin Chishti, Ajmer is the most sacred Muslim pilgrimage site in all of India. Even Hindu rulers make offerings at the tomb of the great saint, acknowledging his blessing as essential to legitimate authority in Rajputana. The city also hosts the only Mughal administrative capital in the Rajput heartland, making it a place where imperial and local power must always negotiate.

Settlement.name: {=!}Udaipur
Settlement.text: {=!}Built by Maharana Udai Singh on the shores of Lake Pichola, Udaipur is the capital of the Sisodia Rajputs of Mewar - the clan that alone among all Rajput houses refused to give their daughters to the Mughal emperors. The Lake Palace and the City Palace form one of the most beautiful architectural ensembles in the world. The city is as much a statement of defiant pride as it is a seat of government.

Settlement.name: {=!}Amber
Settlement.text: {=!}Perched above a narrow valley in the Aravalli hills, the Amber Fort is the ancestral seat of the Kachwaha Rajputs - the most powerful and pragmatic of the Rajput clans. The Kachwahas made their fortunes by serving as the finest generals of the Mughal emperors, and their palaces rival anything in the empire. Sawai Jai Singh II is already planning a new city in the valley below - Jaipur - that will dwarf this ancient capital.

Settlement.name: {=!}Gwalior
Settlement.text: {=!}Sitting atop a sheer sandstone cliff rising from the plains, the Gwalior Fort has been called the pearl of all fortresses in India. Babur himself declared it the only fort worth naming in all of Hindustan. The city that clusters at its foot controls the road between the Mughal heartland and the Deccan, making it eternally and bitterly contested.

Settlement.name: {=!}Jaisalmer
Settlement.text: {=!}Rising from the Thar desert like a mirage made of golden sandstone, Jaisalmer is one of the most isolated and most distinctive cities in India. The Bhati Rajput rulers who built it here depended on the desert itself as their primary defense. The city's remarkable wealth came from the camel caravans of the ancient trade routes between India and Central Asia that passed no other way.

Settlement.name: {=!}Bundi
Settlement.text: {=!}Wedged into a narrow valley in the Aravalli hills, Bundi is the stronghold of the Hada Chauhan Rajputs. Its step-wells are the finest in Rajputana and its tradition of miniature painting has produced some of the most exquisite artwork in the subcontinent. The Taragarh fort that looms above the city has never been taken by assault, its sheer clifftop position making a siege the only possible approach.

Settlement.name: {=!}Bikaner
Settlement.text: {=!}Deep in the Thar desert, Bikaner was founded by the Rathore prince Bika on land so forbidding that no other ruler wanted it. What grew from that unpromising start is a prosperous trading city whose camel-breeding and caravan trade have made it wealthy beyond expectation. The Junagarh fort, built entirely on flat ground without a natural hill to defend it, relies entirely on the excellence of its walls and the reputation of its garrison.

Settlement.name: {=!}Jodhpur
Settlement.text: {=!}The Blue City, whose indigo-washed buildings can be seen from leagues away across the Thar desert, is the capital of the Rathore Rajputs and the largest city in Rajputana. The Mehrangarh Fort that dominates the city from its clifftop position is practically a city in itself, a marvel of Rajput military architecture. The Rathores are among the most militarily aggressive of the Rajput clans, and Jodhpur is their instrument of dominance.

Settlement.name: {=!}Raigadh
Settlement.text: {=!}Raigadh soars above the Konkan clouds on a mesa of sheer basalt, the mountain capital of Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj where the great king was crowned in 1674 with the sacred waters of the seven rivers. The fort is accessible only by a long staircase of 1737 steps cut into the cliff face, and even this single approach is covered by gates and bastions at every turn. The Maratha treasury, the lion throne, and the Jagadishwar temple all stood here at the height of Swarajya's power. To the Marathas, Raigadh is not merely a fort but a symbol of independence from Mughal domination.

Settlement.name: {=!}Pratapgadh
Settlement.text: {=!}Pratapgadh perches on a spur of the Sahyadri where the mountains fall away to the Konkan below, the fort where Shivaji met Bijapur's general Afzal Khan under a truce and slew him with concealed iron tiger-claws in 1659. The ambush that followed routed the Bijapur army and established Shivaji's reputation as a commander of genius. The fort's strategic position above the ghats gives its garrison control of the trade routes between the Deccan plateau and the Konkan ports. Every Maratha soldier knows the story of what happened here, and it fires their courage.

Settlement.name: {=!}Torna
Settlement.text: {=!}Torna was the first fort taken by the young Shivaji in 1645, a sixteen-year-old's audacious seizure of a Bijapur garrison that announced the birth of the Maratha state. The fort's treasury funded Shivaji's initial campaigns, making it the financial cornerstone of Swarajya as much as its symbolic beginning. It stands on a high Sahyadri spur commanding the source of the Velhe River, its walls blending into the basalt rock. For Maratha warriors, to serve at Torna is to serve at the birthplace of their freedom.

Settlement.name: {=!}Panhala
Settlement.text: {=!}Panhala's massive enclosed hill is where Shivaji made his legendary midnight escape in 1660, slipping through the Adilshahi besieging army disguised as an ordinary soldier while his faithful servant Shiva Kashid impersonated him and died in his place. The fort is the largest in the Deccan in terms of enclosed area, with three separate circuits of walls enclosing gardens, tanks, and quarters for a small city. It was Shivaji's preferred residence in the southern Konkan, and the ghost of that desperate night still haunts its three gates. Its fall to any enemy would be a blow as much to morale as to military geography.

Settlement.name: {=!}Shivneri
Settlement.text: {=!}Shivneri is the birthplace of Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj, the sacred hill fort where Jijabai prayed at the shrine of the goddess Shivai for a son who would protect the Maratha people and was granted the warrior she prayed for. The fort is modest compared to Raigadh or Panhala, but its importance to Maratha identity is beyond calculation. Every Maratha pilgrim who climbs to Shivneri is walking in the footsteps of the founder. The garrison here considers themselves custodians of a shrine as much as soldiers of a strategic post.

Settlement.name: {=!}Lohagad
Settlement.text: {=!}Lohagad, the Iron Fort, commands the crest of the Sahyadri above the Bhor Ghat, the main pass between Pune and the Konkan coast. Shivaji used its vast cellars to store the treasure seized from the Mughal viceroy Shaista Khan's train after his famous night raid on Lal Mahal. The fort is connected to its sister fortress Visapur by a ridge that allows defenders to shift between them as needed. Control of Lohagad means control of the most traveled route between the Deccan and the Konkan, where every merchant's pack-bullock pays its toll.

Settlement.name: {=!}Vasai
Settlement.text: {=!}Vasai Fort stands on a peninsula where the Ulhas River meets the sea, the Portuguese Bassein that Shivaji's son Sambhaji besieged for years before the Marathas finally took it in 1739, the most significant defeat of a European power in Indian history to that date. The fort is a testament to Portuguese military architecture, with proper European-style bastions designed to absorb cannon fire from seaward. Its fall broke Portuguese power on the Konkan coast and opened the northern sea approaches to Maratha control. The garrison here must manage a population that still includes Portuguese-descended Christians alongside their Maratha masters.

Settlement.name: {=!}Solapur
Settlement.text: {=!}Solapur's eastern fort guards the gateway between the Deccan plateau and the Marathwada region, a frontier position that has been contested between Bijapur, Hyderabad, and the Marathas for a century. The flat surrounding country offers no natural defensive advantages, so the fort relies entirely on the strength of its masonry and the depth of its ditches. The surrounding territory grows jowar and cotton, and the fort's revenues from taxing the eastern trade routes are considerable. Whoever holds Solapur can threaten either Bijapur or Pune within a few days' march.

Settlement.name: {=!}Pune
Settlement.text: {=!}Nestled in the hills of the Sahyadri ranges on the banks of the Mutha river, Pune is the administrative heart of the Maratha confederacy and the seat of the Peshwas who have surpassed their kings in actual power. The city's economy is built on the constant passage of Maratha armies, and every family of note in the Deccan maintains a house here to be near the source of patronage. Bajirao I has transformed it from a provincial town into the center of a continental power.

Settlement.name: {=!}Kolhapur
Settlement.text: {=!}The seat of the Bhonsle branch that refused to recognize Shahu as Chhatrapati, Kolhapur is a city of proud defiance and ancient temples. The Mahalakshmi temple is one of the six holiest shrines of the goddess in all India, drawing pilgrims from the length of the subcontinent. The city's power rests on the productive lands of the coastal Konkan and its access to the sea through the Western Ghats.

Settlement.name: {=!}Surat
Settlement.text: {=!}Once the greatest port in all of India and the Mughal empire's primary window to the world, Surat's harbor has handled more gold than most kingdoms have seen in a century. The English and Dutch East India Companies maintain their principal factories here, making it a city where European commercial ambition must be managed carefully. Despite Maratha raids that have left parts of the city in ruins, its merchants rebuild each time, for the trade is too valuable to abandon.

Settlement.name: {=!}Satara
Settlement.text: {=!}The formal capital of the Chhatrapati and the seat of the Bhonsle royal family, Satara sits in a high valley of the Sahyadri mountains surrounded by an unbroken ring of formidable hill forts. It is from here that Shahu I nominally rules the Maratha confederacy, though real power has flowed to the Peshwa at Pune. The city's role is as much symbolic as practical - the living embodiment of the Maratha claim to sovereignty.

Settlement.name: {=!}Nashik
Settlement.text: {=!}One of the four sites of the great Kumbh Mela, Nashik on the banks of the Godavari is among the most sacred cities in Maharashtra. Its temples and ghats draw pilgrims year-round, and its religious importance has given it a measure of protection in an era when even sacred places are not safe from armies. Control of Nashik means control of the upper Godavari valley and the main road from the Deccan to the coast.

Settlement.name: {=!}Anandpur Sahib
Settlement.text: {=!}Anandpur Sahib was founded by Guru Tegh Bahadur as a city of refuge and became the birthplace of the Khalsa when Guru Gobind Singh initiated the first Panj Pyare here in 1699, transforming the Sikh community into a martial brotherhood. The fort withstood repeated Mughal and hill-chieftain sieges, and the Guru's forced evacuation of Anandpur in 1705 in the dead of winter is remembered as one of the greatest tragedies of Sikh history. Every Khalsa warrior considers this place holier than any Mughal capital. The garrison here fights with the knowledge that they defend the ground where their order was born.

Settlement.name: {=!}Gobindgarh
Settlement.text: {=!}Gobindgarh Fort commands the approaches to Amritsar and the Golden Temple, its guns covering the roads from every direction. The fort takes its name from Guru Gobind Singh and has been expanded and repaired by successive Sikh Misls who understood that the spiritual center of the Khalsa must be protected by practical military strength. The relationship between the fort's garrison and the Golden Temple's priests is a microcosm of the Sikh balance between the spiritual and the martial. Any army threatening Amritsar must first break Gobindgarh.

Settlement.name: {=!}Attock
Settlement.text: {=!}Attock Fort commands the Indus River at the only practical crossing point for armies between the mountains to the north and the Thar Desert to the south, the gateway from Afghanistan into the Punjab. Akbar built it in 1581 to assert Mughal control over Afghan incursions, and every subsequent power that has contested the Punjab has fought over its river crossing. The fort's walls rise directly from the riverbank, and its guns command the water for hundreds of yards in both directions. To cross at Attock without permission is to invite a cannon ball.

Settlement.name: {=!}Bhatinda
Settlement.text: {=!}Bhatinda Fort rises from the flat Punjab plain with no natural hill to assist its defenses, relying instead on massive walls, deep ditches, and the determination of its garrison. It is one of the oldest forts in the Punjab, claimed to have been built by Raja Bhati of the Bhati clan before the arrival of the Delhi Sultanate. Both Prithviraj Chauhan and the Slave Dynasty sultan Razia used it as a forward base for their Punjab campaigns. The surrounding plain is the heartland of Sikh agricultural settlement, and the fort's garrison must balance military duty with the role of local administrator.

Settlement.name: {=!}Kapurthala
Settlement.text: {=!}Kapurthala's fortified complex serves as the headquarters of the Ahluwalia Misl, one of the most powerful Sikh confederacy units, commanding the fertile lands between the Beas and Sutlej rivers. Jassa Singh Ahluwalia, the celebrated Sikh commander who led the Dal Khalsa to its greatest victories, makes this his base of operations. The fort is prosperous rather than austere, reflecting the Ahluwalia talent for combining military success with commercial wealth. Its garrison is considered among the finest fighting infantry in the Punjab.

Settlement.name: {=!}Kasur
Settlement.text: {=!}Kasur guards the traditional invasion route from Afghanistan into the Punjab heartland, a frontier post that has been attacked more often than any fort in the region. The town and fort have been burned by Afghan raiders and rebuilt by Sikh Misls with such frequency that the locals have developed a fatalistic efficiency in construction. Its position on the main road from Lahore to the Sutlej makes it indispensable to whoever controls the Punjab. A strong Kasur garrison is the first warning any invader from the west receives that the Khalsa is watching.

Settlement.name: {=!}Phillaur
Settlement.text: {=!}Phillaur Fort stands on the south bank of the Sutlej River, commanding the main bridge crossing between Ludhiana and Jalandhar. The fort is well-built and regularly maintained, as the Sutlej is the primary boundary between the territories of different Sikh Misls. Control of Phillaur means control of movement across the most important river boundary in the Punjab confederacy. The garrison here is frequently involved in disputes between neighboring Misls over border crossings and trade tolls rather than fighting external enemies.

Settlement.name: {=!}Jamrud
Settlement.text: {=!}Jamrud Fort crouches at the eastern mouth of the Khyber Pass where the mountains give way to the Peshawar plain, the Sikh fortress that mirrors Attock's Mughal function at a later day. The Sikhs built it to assert their control over the Khyber and deny the Afghans their traditional invasion highway. The garrison lives in a state of permanent vigilance, as the Afridi tribesmen of the pass consider themselves subject to no one and the Afghans beyond them have never accepted Sikh control of their gateway to India. Jamrud is the most dangerous posting in the Punjab.

Settlement.name: {=!}Jhelum
Settlement.text: {=!}Jhelum Fort commands the most difficult river crossing between the Indus and the Chenab, the ancient Hydaspes where Alexander fought his hardest battle against the war elephants of Porus. The current Sikh garrison occupies a structure rebuilt on much older foundations, and the river is still wide and fast here even when not in spate. Control of the Jhelum crossing is essential for any army moving between Kashmir and the Punjab plains. The fort's position also commands the road to the salt mines of Khewra, a source of revenue that makes it commercially as well as militarily vital.

Settlement.name: {=!}Sirhind
Settlement.text: {=!}For generations the seat of the Mughal governor of the Punjab, Sirhind commands the point where the road from Delhi meets the roads to Lahore and Kabul. The battle here where Banda Singh Bahadur avenged the Sikh martyrs is fresh in memory, and the city remains a flashpoint in the perpetual conflict between Mughal authority and Sikh resistance. Its walls and garrison make it the primary obstacle on the road between Delhi and the Punjab heartland.

Settlement.name: {=!}Jalandhar
Settlement.text: {=!}Sitting astride the ancient royal road that has connected Delhi with the northwest frontier since the time of the Mauryas, Jalandhar is the key to the Punjab heartland. Its fertile Doab - the land between two rivers - produces the finest wheat and cotton in the subcontinent. The Sikh Misls regard control of Jalandhar as essential to any serious claim over the Punjab.

Settlement.name: {=!}Lahore
Settlement.text: {=!}The greatest city of the Punjab and the traditional capital of the northwest, Lahore was for centuries the second city of the Mughal empire. The Shalimar gardens, the mosques, and the Badshahi Masjid rival anything in Delhi. Now as Mughal power weakens the city is the prize that every ambitious power - Mughal governor, Afghan raider, and Sikh Misl - simultaneously claims and contests. Holding Lahore is synonymous with ruling the Punjab.

Settlement.name: {=!}Multan
Settlement.text: {=!}Where the five rivers of the Punjab converge into the Indus, Multan has been a trading city since Alexander the Great captured it on his march to the sea. Famous for its Sufi shrines and its cotton trade, it is simultaneously a place of great piety and great commerce. Its position at the southern end of the Punjab makes it the gateway between the subcontinent and Sindh and the overland routes to Persia.

Settlement.name: {=!}Amritsar
Settlement.text: {=!}Founded by Guru Ram Das and made sacred by the Golden Temple at the center of its sacred pool, Amritsar is the spiritual heart of the Sikh nation. The Akal Takht beside the Golden Temple is the seat of Sikh temporal authority, and no leader who wishes to claim legitimacy can ignore its pronouncements. The city is as much a religious institution as a political one, and its defense is considered sacred duty by every Sikh warrior.

Settlement.name: {=!}Sialkot
Settlement.text: {=!}The northernmost major city of the Punjab plain before the land rises into the Himalayan foothills, Sialkot has been a frontier fortress since ancient times. Its craftsmen produce the finest surgical instruments and cutlery in the subcontinent, a trade that has made it prosperous in war and peace alike. Control of Sialkot secures the northeastern approaches to the Punjab from both mountain raiders and armies advancing from Kashmir.

Settlement.name: {=!}Vrindavan
Settlement.text: {=!}Sacred Vrindavan on the Yamuna is the land where Krishna is said to have played in eternal youth, its lanes packed with pilgrims, wandering sadhus, and the music of bhajan singers filling every temple courtyard from dawn to dusk.

Settlement.name: {=!}Govardhan
Settlement.text: {=!}Govardhan village surrounds the sacred hill that Krishna lifted on his finger to shelter the cowherds from Indra's wrath, drawing pilgrims who circumambulate its length on the Braj parikrama as an act of devotion.

Settlement.name: {=!}Barsana
Settlement.text: {=!}Barsana is the hilltop village where Radha was born, the most beloved figure in all of Braj Bhumi, and its famous Lathmar Holi sees women ritually beat the men of Nandgaon with lathis while the crowd roars with laughter.

Settlement.name: {=!}Rohini
Settlement.text: {=!}Rohini is a farming village on the edge of the Delhi ridge where seasonal grain markets and cattle fairs connect the imperial capital with the agricultural communities of the surrounding Doab.

Settlement.name: {=!}Narela
Settlement.text: {=!}Narela is a small market town in the Delhi environs supplying the capital with grain, vegetables, and dairy from the nearby Yamuna floodplain farms.

Settlement.name: {=!}Phulpur
Settlement.text: {=!}Phulpur is known across the empire for its orchards, the local guava so superior in flavour that it commands a premium price in the Allahabad and Agra markets.

Settlement.name: {=!}Jhusi
Settlement.text: {=!}Jhusi sits across the Ganga from Prayag on what was the ancient capital of the Kashi kingdom, its riverside ruins older than memory and its ghats packed with pilgrims crossing to the Triveni Sangam.

Settlement.name: {=!}Malihabad
Settlement.text: {=!}Malihabad is the orchard capital of Hindustan, its mango groves producing fruit of such exceptional quality that the emperor's own table is supplied from here every summer.

Settlement.name: {=!}Kakori
Settlement.text: {=!}Kakori is a weavers' village on the Lucknow road where chikankari hand-embroidery on fine muslin is the hereditary craft, each family's patterns jealously guarded across generations.

Settlement.name: {=!}Kosi
Settlement.text: {=!}Kosi is a cattle market and dairy town on the Mathura road where drovers rest their herds, famous for its milk sweets and the seasonal fairs that draw traders from across the Braj region.

Settlement.name: {=!}Baldev
Settlement.text: {=!}Baldev village is sacred as the abode of Balarama, brother of Krishna, its hilltop temple a constant destination for Vaishnava pilgrims completing the Braj circuit.

Settlement.name: {=!}Nandgaon
Settlement.text: {=!}Nandgaon is the village where the cowherd Nanda Baba raised the infant Krishna, its hill-top temple drawing pilgrims who climb to receive the blessings of the divine foster-father.

Settlement.name: {=!}Nawabganj
Settlement.text: {=!}Nawabganj is a river market town that grew from a waystation established by a local nawab, its ghats busy with boats carrying grain and cotton between Kannauj and the eastern trade routes.

Settlement.name: {=!}Baheri
Settlement.text: {=!}Baheri is a sugarcane village in the fertile Doab where seasonal pressing fills the air with the sweet smoke of jaggery, the golden blocks stacked for sale to the Kannauj merchants.

Settlement.name: {=!}Kirauli
Settlement.text: {=!}Kirauli is a farming village dependent on the garrison of Fatehpur Sikri for its weekly market and the security of its grain stores.

Settlement.name: {=!}Shakar
Settlement.text: {=!}Shakar is a sugarcane-processing settlement that supplied the imperial court at Fatehpur Sikri with jaggery and raw sugar.

Settlement.name: {=!}Koil
Settlement.text: {=!}Koil is the old name for Aligarh town, a market settlement that has grown around the famous fort on the Angan River.

Settlement.name: {=!}Iglas
Settlement.text: {=!}Iglas is an agricultural village in the Aligarh district known for its brass and bell-metal wares traded in the Agra and Delhi markets.

Settlement.name: {=!}Chandausi
Settlement.text: {=!}Chandausi is a trading town on the edge of the Moradabad district where the weekly market serves the surrounding cotton and grain farms.

Settlement.name: {=!}Naugawan
Settlement.text: {=!}Naugawan is a sugar-producing village in the Sambhal hinterland whose seasonal jaggery markets draw merchants from the surrounding Rohilkhand countryside.

Settlement.name: {=!}Hapur
Settlement.text: {=!}Hapur is a grain market on the Delhi-Moradabad road where the wheat and cotton of the western Doab are assembled for the capital's merchants.

Settlement.name: {=!}Sardhana
Settlement.text: {=!}Sardhana was the seat of the Begum Samru, a former dancing girl who became a queen, her Catholic church an incongruous monument in the heart of the Mughal countryside.

Settlement.name: {=!}Bithur
Settlement.text: {=!}Bithur is a sacred town on the Ganga where the sage Valmiki is said to have written the Ramayana, its ghats packed with pilgrims on the auspicious bathing days.

Settlement.name: {=!}Bilhaur
Settlement.text: {=!}Bilhaur is an agricultural market village on the Ganga's southern bank supplying grain and vegetables to the Kanpur fort garrison.

Settlement.name: {=!}Makrandpur
Settlement.text: {=!}Makrandpur is a farming village on the Ganga plain whose revenues from wheat and sugarcane support the Kannauj administration.

Settlement.name: {=!}Tirwa
Settlement.text: {=!}Tirwa is an agricultural settlement in the Farrukhabad district known for its potatoes and the seasonal fairs where local zamindars display their livestock.

Settlement.name: {=!}Mainpuri
Settlement.text: {=!}Mainpuri is a district market town whose revenue from the surrounding grain farms has made it the administrative center of the middle Doab.

Settlement.name: {=!}Auraiya
Settlement.text: {=!}Auraiya is a riverside village on the Yamuna where boatmen ferry goods and pilgrims across the river between the towns of the eastern Doab.

Settlement.name: {=!}Amroha
Settlement.text: {=!}Amroha is a textile and grain market town in Rohilkhand whose weekly market connects the agricultural villages with the merchants of Moradabad.

Settlement.name: {=!}Thakurdwara
Settlement.text: {=!}Thakurdwara is a temple village on the Ramganga whose Shiva shrine draws pilgrims from the surrounding Moradabad district.

Settlement.name: {=!}Mehrauli
Settlement.text: {=!}Mehrauli is the village that grew around the Qutb Minar, the 800-year-old minaret that still towers over the southern Delhi countryside.

Settlement.name: {=!}Badarpur
Settlement.text: {=!}Badarpur is a limestone and brick-producing settlement on the Yamuna whose quarries have supplied construction material to Delhi for centuries.

Settlement.name: {=!}Narayanganj
Settlement.text: {=!}Narayanganj is a river port where the Buriganga and Lakshya rivers meet, its wharves perpetually busy with boats loading jute and the finest muslin for the downstream markets of the delta.

Settlement.name: {=!}Demra
Settlement.text: {=!}Demra is the home of the legendary Jamdani weavers who produce muslin finer than running water, their fingers moving through threads so delicate that a full sari can be folded into a matchbox.

Settlement.name: {=!}Jiaganj
Settlement.text: {=!}Jiaganj is a silk-weaving settlement opposite Murshidabad where the famous Baluchari saris with their mythological border scenes are woven on hand-looms by families who have practiced the craft for generations.

Settlement.name: {=!}Lalbagh
Settlement.text: {=!}Lalbagh is the garden suburb of Murshidabad where the Nawab's officials maintain their estates among flowering orchards watered by the Bhagirathi, a green refuge from the city's noise.

Settlement.name: {=!}Baharampur
Settlement.text: {=!}Baharampur is a garrison town supporting the Nawab's Bengal administration, its barracks and storehouses the logistical backbone of the army that maintains the peace of the province.

Settlement.name: {=!}Chandernagore
Settlement.text: {=!}Chandernagore maintains its air of alien orderliness as a French trading post on the Hooghly, its warehouses stacked with indigo and cotton while European ships ride at anchor in the stream.

Settlement.name: {=!}Serampore
Settlement.text: {=!}Serampore is a small Danish settlement on the Hooghly that trades in textiles and indigo, its compact European quarter a curiosity for the Bengali villagers who supply its warehouses.

Settlement.name: {=!}Hajipur
Settlement.text: {=!}Hajipur is famous for its sweet litchi orchards that supply the Mughal courts of Bihar, its fruit so perishable and so prized that relays of horses carry it north to Delhi in the harvest season.

Settlement.name: {=!}Fatuha
Settlement.text: {=!}Fatuha is a riverside market village on the Gandak where saltpeter boats from the Bihar interior stop to take on provisions before the river journey to the great entrepot of Patna.

Settlement.name: {=!}Danapur
Settlement.text: {=!}Danapur is the great cantonment on the Ganga's south bank where the Bengal Nawab stations his river flotilla and gunboat crews who patrol the Gangetic trade lanes between Patna and Calcutta, its barracks perpetually busy with armed boatmen who double as river police for the grain merchants.

Settlement.name: {=!}Comilla
Settlement.text: {=!}Comilla is an important market town in eastern Bengal where the trade routes from Tripura and the Chittagong hills converge with the river traffic of the great Meghna system.

Settlement.name: {=!}Noakhali
Settlement.text: {=!}Noakhali is a silt-island village in the unstable Meghna delta where fishing communities and river farmers live on land that the next flood season may reclaim entirely.

Settlement.name: {=!}Tangi
Settlement.text: {=!}Tangi is a pottery village on the Mahanadi's western bank producing the distinctive red earthenware of Orissa, traded downriver to the coastal markets by the boat-load.

Settlement.name: {=!}Athgarh
Settlement.text: {=!}Athgarh's zamindars control the ferry crossing over the Mahanadi, extracting tolls from merchants and pilgrims who need to cross between Cuttack and the interior districts.

Settlement.name: {=!}Banki
Settlement.text: {=!}Banki is a timber and bamboo market at the hill forest edge where sal and teak are brought for the construction trades of the Orissan coast and the boat-builders of the delta.

Settlement.name: {=!}Karwan
Settlement.text: {=!}Karwan is a historical settlement and market area on the outskirts of Hyderabad where caravans from the north traditionally set up camp before entering the city.

Settlement.name: {=!}Saidabad
Settlement.text: {=!}Saidabad is a Hyderabad suburb village where the noble families of the Nizam's court maintain their garden estates away from the city's heat and noise.

Settlement.name: {=!}Deogiri
Settlement.text: {=!}Deogiri is the older Yadava name for the impregnable Deccan fortress that became Daulatabad, its ancient history buried beneath successive layers of Islamic fortification.

Settlement.name: {=!}Sultanpur
Settlement.text: {=!}Sultanpur is a market settlement near Daulatabad whose bazaars supply the fortress garrison with grain, fodder, and the provisions a large military force requires.

Settlement.name: {=!}Osmanabad
Settlement.text: {=!}Osmanabad is the district market town of the Latur plateau region, its grain and cotton fairs serving the agricultural communities of the Marathwada interior.

Settlement.name: {=!}Tuljapur
Settlement.text: {=!}Tuljapur is a sacred village whose Tulja Bhavani temple is one of the three-and-a-half divine seats of Maharashtra, drawing pilgrims from every corner of the Maratha lands.

Settlement.name: {=!}Latur
Settlement.text: {=!}Latur is a market town in the Marathwada district where cotton and jowar from the black soil plateau are traded in markets that have operated since the Bahmani era.

Settlement.name: {=!}Ausa
Settlement.text: {=!}Ausa is a market village near a substantial Bijapur-era fort on the Marathwada plateau, its garrison market supplying the soldiers with necessities from the surrounding countryside.

Settlement.name: {=!}Kinwat
Settlement.text: {=!}Kinwat is a forest-edge village on the Godavari tributary in the Adilabad district where the jungle produce of bamboo, timber, and medicinal plants is traded.

Settlement.name: {=!}Digras
Settlement.text: {=!}Digras is a cotton-market village in the Yavatmal district where the white gold of the Vidarbha plain is assembled for the merchants of Nagpur.

Settlement.name: {=!}Sholapur
Settlement.text: {=!}Sholapur is an important cotton-weaving town on the eastern Deccan plateau where the powerloom textile industry has deep roots in the hand-loom tradition.

Settlement.name: {=!}Mudhol
Settlement.text: {=!}Mudhol is a small princely settlement in the Karnataka Deccan whose chiefs maintain nominal loyalty to the Maratha confederacy while governing their estates independently.

Settlement.name: {=!}Basavakalyan
Settlement.text: {=!}Basavakalyan is a Lingayat pilgrimage town in the Bidar district sacred to the twelfth-century Basavanna, the philosopher who challenged caste through his vachana devotional poetry.

Settlement.name: {=!}Humnabad
Settlement.text: {=!}Humnabad is a market village on the Karnataka Deccan where grain and cotton from the Gulbarga district are traded in the weekly markets.

Settlement.name: {=!}Narsapur
Settlement.text: {=!}Narsapur is a lace-making village on the Godavari delta where the bobbin lace craft introduced by Portuguese missionaries has become the hereditary occupation of the Telugu fishing community.

Settlement.name: {=!}Siddipet
Settlement.text: {=!}Siddipet is a weavers' village in the Telangana countryside where the hand-loom cotton cloth is produced for the Hyderabad markets.

Settlement.name: {=!}Hanamkonda
Settlement.text: {=!}Hanamkonda preserves the thousand-pillar Kakatiya temple and the memory of a dynasty that challenged Delhi, its carved basalt columns still standing in the village fields.

Settlement.name: {=!}Kazipet
Settlement.text: {=!}Kazipet is a market village servicing the cloth trade between Warangal's workshops and the merchants of the Deccan plateau, its weekly market drawing traders from a wide radius.

Settlement.name: {=!}Narsampet
Settlement.text: {=!}Narsampet is a weaving settlement on the plateau country east of Warangal where the local cotton is worked into coarse cloth and turbans for the Deccan market, its small bazaar trading also in the iron hoes and ploughshares forged in the village charcoal furnaces.

Settlement.name: {=!}Paithan
Settlement.text: {=!}Paithan on the Godavari was the Satavahana capital Pratishthana two thousand years ago, and its weavers still produce the famous Paithani silk with the golden borders that decorate every aristocratic wardrobe in the Deccan.

Settlement.name: {=!}Khuldabad
Settlement.text: {=!}Khuldabad is the valley of saints, its Sufi dargahs drawing pilgrims from across the Deccan, and it is here that Aurangzeb was buried in simple earth as he commanded, no tomb to mark an emperor's grave.

Settlement.name: {=!}Gangapur City
